questions.question: What best distinguishes respiratory distress from respiratory failure?
options: A. Distress means compensating with adequate oxygenation/mentation; failure means compensation has failed
B. Distress always requires intubation; failure never does
C. Distress only occurs in infants; failure only in adolescents
D. There is no clinical difference
questions.rationale: Respiratory distress is a compensated state (increased work of breathing but adequate oxygenation/mentation); respiratory failure is a decompensated state where oxygenation/ventilation is now inadequate.

questions.question: Which finding is classic for upper airway obstruction?
options: A. Wheezing
B. Stridor
C. Crackles
D. Irregular rate with no abnormal sound
questions.rationale: Upper airway obstruction (e.g., croup, foreign body, epiglottitis) classically produces stridor.

questions.question: Which finding is classic for lower airway disease?
options: A. Stridor
B. Wheezing with prolonged expiration
C. Grunting
D. Lethargy alone
questions.rationale: Lower airway disease (e.g., asthma, bronchiolitis) classically produces wheezing with prolonged expiration.

questions.question: Which findings are classic for lung tissue (parenchymal) disease?
options: A. Stridor and barking cough
B. Wheezing only
C. Grunting, crackles, and diminished breath sounds
D. No abnormal sounds at all
questions.rationale: Parenchymal disease (e.g., pneumonia, pulmonary edema) classically produces grunting, crackles, and diminished breath sounds.

questions.question: What characterizes disordered control of breathing?
options: A. Stridor with barking cough
B. Wheezing with prolonged expiration
C. An irregular rate/pattern without the classic sounds of the other three categories
D. Grunting and crackles
questions.rationale: Disordered control of breathing (from a CNS problem, toxin, or neuromuscular disease) causes an irregular rate/pattern without the classic sounds seen in the other three categories.

questions.question: A child who was struggling hard to breathe now appears to have less work of breathing. What should this prompt?
options: A. Immediate reassurance that the child is improving
B. Suspicion of exhaustion/impending failure rather than automatic reassurance
C. Discharge from EMS care
D. No further assessment is needed
questions.rationale: Decreasing work of breathing in a previously struggling child can reflect exhaustion rather than improvement — a dangerous, easily misread sign requiring close reassessment.

questions.question: Which PAT change suggests impending respiratory failure?
options: A. Appearance shifting from anxious/agitated to lethargic
B. Appearance shifting from lethargic to anxious
C. Improved skin color
D. No PAT change is relevant
questions.rationale: A shift in Appearance from anxious/agitated to lethargic on repeat PAT is a warning sign of impending respiratory failure.

questions.question: Should a provider wait for SpO2 to drop before escalating respiratory support?
options: A. Yes, SpO2 is the only reliable indicator
B. No — other findings often precede a measurable SpO2 drop and should prompt earlier escalation
C. Only in infants
D. Only during interfacility transport
questions.rationale: Grunting, decreasing WOB after struggling, and PAT Appearance changes often precede a measurable drop in SpO2 and should prompt escalation before saturation falls.

questions.question: What is appropriate field management for respiratory distress (not failure)?
options: A. Immediate bag-mask ventilation for all patients
B. Positioning of comfort, supplemental oxygen, and category-targeted treatment with close monitoring
C. No treatment until arrival at the hospital
D. Immediate advanced airway placement
questions.rationale: Distress is managed with positioning of comfort, oxygen, and treatment targeted to the likely category, with close monitoring for transition to failure.

questions.question: What is appropriate field management for respiratory failure?
options: A. Positioning of comfort and observation only
B. Assisted ventilation (BVM with high-flow oxygen) and rapid transport
C. Withholding oxygen until intubation
D. Discharge at scene
questions.rationale: Respiratory failure requires assisted ventilation with high-flow oxygen and rapid transport; PEPP frames deeper airway/arrest management as PALS-reviewer content.

questions.question: Where does PEPP direct providers for drug-assisted airway management and arrest-specific ventilation parameters?
options: A. The BLS reviewer
B. The PALS reviewer
C. This is fully covered in PEPP itself
D. No resource is referenced
questions.rationale: PEPP frames respiratory failure management as field recognition and initial stabilization, deferring drug-assisted airway management and arrest-specific ventilation depth to the PALS reviewer.

questions.question: Why are respiratory problems especially significant in pediatric prehospital care?
options: A. They are the leading pathway to pediatric cardiac arrest
B. They never progress to failure in children
C. They are less common in children than adults
D. They have no relationship to cardiac arrest in children
questions.rationale: Unlike adults, where a primary cardiac event is more common, respiratory problems are the leading pathway to pediatric cardiac arrest, making early recognition high-yield.

questions.question: Why is categorizing a respiratory problem by mechanism (upper airway, lower airway, tissue, control) clinically useful?
options: A. It has no treatment implications
B. It guides both expected exam findings and which field treatment is appropriate
C. It only matters for documentation
D. It replaces the need for a primary assessment
questions.rationale: Categorizing by mechanism helps providers anticipate exam findings and select the appropriate targeted treatment (e.g., bronchodilator for lower airway disease).

questions.question: A toddler has stridor and a barking cough. Which category of respiratory compromise is most likely?
options: A. Lower airway disease
B. Upper airway obstruction
C. Lung tissue disease
D. Disordered control of breathing
questions.rationale: Stridor with a barking cough is classic for upper airway obstruction, such as croup.

questions.question: An infant has an irregular breathing pattern with no wheeze, stridor, grunting, or crackles, following a suspected toxic ingestion. Which category fits best?
options: A. Upper airway obstruction
B. Lower airway disease
C. Lung tissue disease
D. Disordered control of breathing
questions.rationale: An irregular rate/pattern without classic sounds, especially with a toxin as the suspected cause, fits disordered control of breathing.
